description: A palm nut soup from the Niger Delta — rich, orange, fragrant with banga spices that exist in no other Nigerian soup. Completely different from every other soup in this library. Pairs with starch, eba, or white rice.

ingredients.name: palm fruits (fresh or canned)
ingredients.quantity: 1.5kg fresh or 800ml canned palm nut cream
ingredients.note: Fresh palm fruits give the best flavour — boil, pound, and extract the cream yourself. Canned palm nut cream (tinned oroma) is a perfectly acceptable shortcut and cuts the prep time significantly.

ingredients.name: beef or goat meat
ingredients.note: Goat meat is traditional in banga soup and handles the bold spices beautifully. Beef works well too.

ingredients.name: stockfish (okporoko)
ingredients.note: Soak 30 minutes. Debone. Important for depth.

ingredients.name: dried banga spice (oburunbebe stick)
ingredients.note: This is the defining flavour of banga soup — a dried bark with a deeply aromatic, slightly medicinal, irreplaceable taste. Available in Nigerian grocery stores. No substitute exists.

ingredients.name: banga leaves (obenetete / atama leaves)
ingredients.quantity: 1 tbsp dried or a small handful fresh
ingredients.note: Dried banga leaves available at Nigerian stores. Adds a distinctive herbal note specific to this soup. Skip if completely unavailable — the soup is still good without it.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper (ata rodo)
ingredients.note: Blend or pound. Banga soup is moderately spiced — 2 is the sweet spot.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: 1 for parboiling meat, 1 chopped for the soup.

ingredients.name: ground crayfish
ingredients.note: Adds the savoury backbone underneath the banga spices.

ingredients.name: locust beans (iru/dawadawa)
ingredients.note: Rinse briefly. Works well with the palm nut base.

ingredients.name: dried uziza leaves
ingredients.note: A peppery, aromatic leaf added at the end. Pairs beautifully with banga. Skip if unavailable.

ingredients.name: meat stock
ingredients.note: From parboiling the meat. Used to adjust consistency.

ingredients.name: salt
ingredients.note: Season at the end — the palm cream and fish contribute salt.

steps.title: Extract Palm Nut Cream (if using fresh)
steps.instruction: If using fresh palm fruits — wash and place in a large pot. Cover with water and boil over high heat for 30–40 minutes until the outer pulp is completely soft and the fruits can be squeezed easily. Drain. Pound the boiled fruits in a mortar in batches until the pulp separates from the fibres and nuts. Add warm water gradually while pounding to help extraction. Squeeze and strain the resulting mixture through a sieve or cheesecloth to extract the thick orange cream. Discard fibres. If using canned palm nut cream — open, stir well, and proceed to step 2.
steps.sensory_cue: Freshly extracted palm nut cream should be thick, deeply orange, and smell sweet and slightly fruity — like fresh palm wine mixed with oil. It should coat the back of a spoon.
steps.failure_risk: Under-boiling the palm fruits makes extraction nearly impossible — the pulp will not release from the fibres. The fruits need to be completely soft before pounding.

steps.title: Prepare Proteins and Stock
steps.instruction: Season meat generously with salt, seasoning cube, and onion. Parboil until 75% done using minimal water — reserve the rich stock. Fry parboiled meat until golden-brown. Soak and debone stockfish. Debone and flake smoked fish. Have all proteins organised and ready. Blend or pound scotch bonnet separately.
steps.sensory_cue: Fried meat deeply golden. Stock dark and rich. All proteins deboned and ready.
steps.failure_risk: Goat meat takes significantly longer to become tender than beef. If using goat, allow the full 60 minutes for parboiling and check tenderness before frying.

steps.title: Cook the Palm Nut Cream Base
steps.instruction: Pour the palm nut cream into a large pot. Add 1 cup of meat stock and 1 cup of water. Bring to a boil over medium-high heat, stirring regularly. Add the banga spice sticks or ground banga spice. Add chopped onion. Stir and let the palm cream cook, boiling actively, for 15–20 minutes. It will reduce and deepen in colour and the distinctive banga aroma will develop and intensify.
steps.sensory_cue: The palm cream cooking with the banga spice produces one of the most distinctive aromas in Nigerian cooking — deeply aromatic, slightly woody, sweet, and complex. The cream should reduce to a thick, deeply orange soup base. You will know the banga spice is working when the kitchen fills with that specific fragrance.
steps.failure_risk: Insufficient cooking of the palm cream at this stage leaves it tasting raw and oily. It needs to actively boil and reduce before the proteins go in. The oil should begin separating and floating on top when it is ready.

steps.title: Add Crayfish, Aromatics and Proteins
steps.instruction: Add ground crayfish and locust beans to the reduced palm cream. Stir well. Add blended scotch bonnet. Add the fried meat, stockfish, and smoked fish. Stir gently to coat everything in the orange palm cream base. Add crushed seasoning cubes. Bring back to a gentle boil.
steps.sensory_cue: The soup at this stage should look thick, deeply orange, and full of protein. The banga spice aroma should be prominent. Adding crayfish to the palm cream creates a layered, complex savoury smell that is distinctive to this soup.
steps.failure_risk: If the soup looks too thin at this stage, let it boil uncovered for 5 more minutes before adding protein. Adding meat to thin soup dilutes it further and it will never thicken properly.

steps.title: Simmer, Season and Add Leaves
steps.instruction: Cover and simmer on low heat for 20–25 minutes, stirring every 5 minutes. The soup will thicken further as it cooks. Taste and add salt now. If using dried banga leaves, add them now and stir in — they will soften in the heat. If using dried uziza leaves, add them in the final 5 minutes. Taste one final time — the flavour should be deeply complex, aromatic, and uniquely different from any other Nigerian soup.
steps.sensory_cue: Finished banga soup is thick, deeply orange, and has a glossy surface from the natural palm oil. The aroma should carry the banga spice clearly — aromatic, slightly sweet, deeply savoury. One taste should be unlike any other soup you have made — that is correct.
steps.failure_risk: Uziza leaves are peppery and strong — add only a small amount and taste before adding more. Too much uziza makes the soup aggressively peppery and overwhelms the delicate banga spice flavour.
